excerpt: Through the continuous sanctification and exaltation of the angels, a profound effect was established in them: they became incapable of error, attaining thereby a state of absolute purity.
source_authority: Qadi al-Nuʿman on the states of angels and their incapacity for error
source_location_hint: References to tasbih and angelic sanctification

excerpt: After a believer's death, the account of deeds ceases, since action is bound to the body. Yet the yearning for knowledge persists; wherefore the believer is granted knowledge in the Temple.
source_authority: Kirmani on the persistence of knowledge after death and the believer's continued spiritual advancement
source_location_hint: Teachings on the posthumous state and knowledge acquisition

excerpt: The son of each Imam—he destined to become Imam himself—is a prophet. Praise for believers is the opposite: praise belongs to God, signifying that God is the present Imam and praise is his successor-son.
source_authority: Kirmani on the relationship between successive Imams and the transfer of knowledge
source_location_hint: Exegesis of the Hamd and succession doctrine

excerpt: None of the limits of the summons could become an Imam while advancing—only the Imam's son possesses this capacity. He alone has authority extending from heaven to earth.
source_authority: Kirmani on the exclusive capacity of the designated successor
source_location_hint: Doctrinal teaching on ḥudūd and succession

excerpt: The existence of Ḥasan and Ḥusayn is impossible without ʿAlī the Great, nor can they together rival ʿAlī the Great.
source_authority: Kirmani's doctrinal hierarchy of the ʿAlid figures
source_location_hint: Teachings on the supremacy of ʿAlī and subordination of his sons

excerpt: It comprises nine parts, and the number nine signifies the extremity, perfection, and essence of the unit—as the month of Ramadan, being the ninth month, crowns all the other months.
source_authority: Qadi al-Nuʿman on numerical symbolism and the nine divisions of Ayat al-Kursi

excerpt: God, in truth, embodies the essence of Lā ilāha illā Huwa (There is no god but He), whose numerical value is 110—the value corresponding to ʿAlī.
source_authority: al-Sijistani on numerical correlations and divine names
source_location_hint: Esoteric numerology and the divine attributes

excerpt: In the second Islam, the condition is one of dignity and lordship, for which he commanded his children not to depart life until they had entrusted their accounts to their superiors.
source_authority: Nasir-i Khusraw on the hierarchy of Islamic submission and the testamentary succession
source_location_hint: Teaching on taqwa and the ranks of Islam
